title: Test harness: simulated time, activity mocking, replay assertions
purpose: Ship the Temporal-equivalent test harness so authors can test workflows without an engine. Simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities with canned typed results, and assert a workflow replays to the same observations — all in pure gleam test.
task: Implement aion/testing and its submodules: a TestEnv binding the ffi surface to in-Gleam test implementations (no beamr, engine, or store); testing/clock.gleam (advance resolves pending sleeps/timers instantly); testing/mock.gleam (activity mock registry intercepting workflow.run); testing/replay.gleam (run-twice-over-history replay assertions). Author workflow code must be unchanged between test and production.

requirements.title: TestEnv and the test-side ffi binding
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a TestEnv that runs entirely under gleam test with no beamr, engine, or store, binding the ffi surface to in-Gleam test implementations so author workflow code is unchanged between test and production.
acceptance: aion/testing exposes a TestEnv constructed in a test, holding the simulated clock, the mock registry, and the observation capture, stored in process-scoped state (process dictionary or an ETS table keyed by the running pid) so concurrent tests do not collide.

The swap mechanism is concrete: a hand-written test-only Erlang module test/aion_flow_ffi.erl implements every binding the production NIF namespace 'aion_flow_ffi' exposes, in-process against the current process's TestEnv. gleam test compiles and loads it so the identical @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", ...) declarations resolve to it; the package ships NO aion_flow_ffi module in src/ (in production the engine registers those NIFs at runtime, per D2). Author workflow code and every @external declaration are byte-identical between test and production — only which module backs the name at load time differs.
A doc comment states the harness requires no external services (CO11) and is the recommended way to test workflows.
A test runs a trivial workflow under a TestEnv and asserts it completes with the expected typed result.

requirements.title: Simulated clock
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a simulated clock whose advance(Duration) resolves pending sleeps and timers instantly, with no wall-clock wait.
acceptance: testing/clock.gleam implements a logical clock; advance(env, Duration) moves logical time forward and resolves any sleep/timer whose deadline is now reached, returning control with no wall-clock sleep.
workflow.now under the harness returns the simulated clock's current logical time (deterministically), not wall time.
A test sleeps for days(30), advances the clock, and asserts the workflow proceeds instantly (the test itself runs in milliseconds).

requirements.title: Activity mock registry
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define an activity mock registry whose mock(name, fn(input) -> Result(output, ActivityError)) intercepts workflow.run for that activity and returns the canned typed result without dispatching a NIF.
acceptance: testing/mock.gleam lets a test register mock(env, name, fn(input) -> Result(output, ActivityError)); when the workflow calls workflow.run on an activity of that name, the mock's typed result is returned instead of a NIF dispatch.
The mock is type-checked against the activity's input/output types so a wrong-typed mock fails to compile.
A test mocks an activity to return a canned result and asserts the workflow under the env observes it; a second mocks a Retryable failure and asserts the workflow sees the typed ActivityError.

requirements.title: Replay assertions
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a replay assertion that runs a workflow over a recorded observation sequence twice and asserts the two observation sequences are identical, catching accidental non-determinism.
acceptance: testing/replay.gleam captures the observation sequence of a workflow run under the env, replays the workflow against that captured history, and asserts the replayed observation sequence is identical.

A test with a deterministic workflow passes the replay assertion; a test with a deliberately non-deterministic workflow (e.g. branching on an un-recorded value) fails the assertion with a clear diagnostic.
A doc comment relates the assertion to AD's non-determinism detection: the harness surfaces in test what the engine would surface as a NonDeterminismError in production.

boundaries: SHALL NOT require a running engine, beamr, or store — the harness is pure Gleam (CO11).
SHALL NOT implement the production engine replay/determinism — it simulates the observable contract for tests; the real machinery is AD.
SHALL NOT change the author-facing API; workflow code is identical in test and production.
verification: gleam test passes for the clock, mock, and replay-assertion tests, all running with no external services and in milliseconds (no wall-clock waits).
No panic/todo; a non-deterministic workflow fails the replay assertion with a diagnostic.
